summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #6 in Amsterdam, reaffirming DuckDB's mission to make large datasets approachable rather than intimidating. He presents evidence that single-node computing power has outpaced the growth of useful data sizes, citing analysis showing P99.9 of data warehouse sizes is under 300GB. He shares adoption metrics including 32 million monthly extension installs and 1.8 million monthly website visitors. The talk transitions to Mark discussing upcoming v1.2 features and the lakehouse focus for the year ahead, followed by Q&A covering architecture, Wasm, partitioning, and extension development.
key_points: Single-node computing capability has grown faster than useful dataset sizes, with P99.9 of Snowflake/Redshift datasets under 300GB
DuckDB ran TPC-H scale factor 300 on a Raspberry Pi and scale factor 30,000 on a 96-core machine
Adoption metrics: 32 million monthly extension installs, 1.8 million monthly unique website visitors
Lakehouse support is the main focus for the coming year, with partitioning support planned
The team believes the engine architecture is sound and wouldn't change it even with double the headcount—more people would go toward client integrations
money_quotes: The intuition is really to move from this world where people generally fear large data sets because they make everything more complicated to a world where you are no longer scared of this big pile of CSV files.
The P99.9 of data set sizes was under 300 gigabyte, which is really shocking if you think about it after a decade of talking about big data.
He has shown that we can actually run the industry standard benchmark TPC-H with a scale factor of 300, which is roughly equivalent to 300 gigabytes of input data on a Raspberry Pi.
We accidentally made one of the bigger websites of this country. I think we have beaten the Lidl at this point.
The actual engine work, we don't think, could scale better. So that's something that we could put more people on.
A tool that charges you 1,000 euros per person per year is going to have to be much, much better against one that doesn't cost anything, just saying.
The capabilities of a single node in terms of processing data have essentially risen quicker than the growth of useful data sets.
key_insight: The era of big data infrastructure is largely unnecessary for most workloads, as single-node hardware with efficient software like DuckDB can handle 99.9% of real-world analytical datasets.
